file_name: Ashtagi et al. - 2025 - Melanoma dermoscopic image segmentation by analyzing color features using active contour model.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an Active Contour Model (ACM) combined with machine learning (ML) techniques for melanoma dermoscopic image segmentation. It explicitly states the integration of ACM with ML to enhance segmentation accuracy and lesion pattern recognition, evaluated on the ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel Active Contour Model (ACM) combined with machine learning for melanoma dermoscopic image segmentation. It includes a methodology section detailing the mathematical modeling, describes the use of specific datasets (PH2 and ISIC 2017), and reports quantitative experimental results (accuracy, Dice coefficient, Jaccard index) comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques. The structure follows a standard research format: Introduction, Methodology, Results, and Conclusion.

title: Melanoma Dermoscopic Image Segmentation by Analyzing Color Features Using Active Contour Model
brief_summary: This study proposes a robust dermoscopic lesion image segmentation process for melanoma identification by integrating an Active Contour Model (ACM) with machine learning techniques. The method utilizes a Gaussian distribution model to manage pixel intensity changes and establishes strong associations between foreground and background entities to improve pixel detection and edge formation. The proposed ACM was evaluated on the ISIC 2017 challenge dataset and the PH2 dataset for validation. Performance was assessed using segmentation accuracy, Dice coefficient, and Jaccard index metrics. Experimental results on the PH2 dataset demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques, achieving a segmentation accuracy of 96.77%, a Dice coefficient of 93.97%, and a Jaccard index of 88.95%. The findings indicate that the fusion of ACM and ML effectively enhances segmentation quality and supports early melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is melanoma dermoscopic image segmentation. The paper aims to differentiate between melanoma and non-melanoma instances using the ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets.

The study differentiates between melanoma and non-melanoma instances. The PH2 dataset typically contains melanoma, benign nevus, and seborrheic keratosis, while ISIC 2017 contains melanoma and various benign/malignant classes, but the paper frames the task as melanoma vs. non-melanoma segmentation.
Mentioned in the introduction regarding incidence statistics (3 million Americans affected annually) but not studied or segmented in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction regarding incidence statistics but not studied or segmented in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic lesion images (melanoma and non-melanoma regions)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an Active Contour Model (ACM) for dermoscopic lesion image segmentation. The primary output is a segmented mask distinguishing foreground (lesion) from background. Performance is evaluated using segmentation accuracy, Dice coefficient, and Jaccard index on the ISIC 2017 and PH2 datasets. While the paper mentions classification as a downstream step or context, the core contribution and reported metrics are for segmentation.

The paper mentions that segmentation is a prerequisite for classification and references using ML algorithms (FCN, AlexNet, etc.) for classification in the methodology overview, but the experimental results and primary focus are on the segmentation task itself.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic lesion image dataset used to validate the performance of the proposed active contour model. It contains images with accurate and sharp edges.

use.validation: Used to validate the performance of the proposed active contour model.
use.testing: Used for performance evaluation (validation set role in this context).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 lesion segmentation dataset, formed through expert observations to provide automated solutions for skin lesion segmentation and differentiation between melanoma and non-melanoma.

use.training: The proposed active contour model was trained upon the training set (2000 images).
use.validation: Performance results were evaluated using the training set (as stated in text) and the validation set (150 images) is part of the dataset structure.
use.testing: Performance was evaluated on the testing set (600 images) and the dataset was used to assess the performance of the suggested ACM.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed Active Contour Model and state-of-the-art techniques were based on quantitative metrics (segmentation accuracy, Dice coefficient, Jaccard index, sensitivity, specificity) without statistical significance testing.

PH2 Dataset.notes: The proposed ACM demonstrated superior performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques on the PH2 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Active Contour Model (ACM) integrated with Machine Learning (ML) techniques

architecture_details.core_method: Active Contour Model (ACM) utilizing Gaussian distribution model for pixel intensity control and establishing associations between foreground and background entities.
architecture_details.segmentation_approach: Lesion images are split into multiple patches. Unary potentials and pairwise potentials are used for accurate findings. Gaussian distribution model T=(K, S) is utilized to form segmented structures with sharp lesion edges S and vertices K.
architecture_details.energy_model: Combination of unary potential Sj, pairwise potential Sy, and color band energy Se.
architecture_details.preprocessing: Image enhancement using CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) and Wiener Filter for blurring reduction.
architecture_details.classification_components: The system includes a classification phase using multiple ML/DL architectures: FCN, AlexNet, FCN-32s, FCN-16s, FCN-8s, Deeplab V3+, Mask R-CNN, and Ensemble-L.
architecture_details.probability_estimation: Multi-class probability estimation using Gaussian distribution model where class label mb = arg y(u).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Features extracted from segmented images using Local Binary Pattern, Histogram of Oriented Gradients, Edge Histogram, and Gabor techniques (referenced in literature survey context, but ACM is the proposed method).

training_details.loss_function: Energy minimization in the proposed active contour model (specific loss function not named, but energy model defined by unary, pairwise, and color band energies).
training_details.implementation_environment: 64-bit Windows 10 OS, 16 GB RAM, Intel processor.

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Segmented mask (binary or multi-class depending on patch labeling) and classification labels (Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma).
classification_or_output_head.classification_models: FCN, AlexNet, FCN-32s, FCN-16s, FCN-8s, Deeplab V3+, Mask R-CNN, Ensemble-L (used for the classification phase of the pipeline).

preprocessing_techniques: Image Enhancement using CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization)
Pixel detection for background and foreground
Lesion images split into multiple patches
conclusions: The proposed Active Contour Model (ACM) integrated with machine learning techniques effectively manages high-dimensional lesion image data and improves pixel detection performance by establishing strong associations between foreground and background entities.
The use of a Gaussian distribution model allows the ACM to control changes in pixel intensity across multiple color bands, resulting in sharp edges and improved lesion pattern recognition.

The proposed ACM demonstrated superior segmentation performance compared to state-of-the-art techniques on the PH2 dataset, achieving a segmentation accuracy of 96.77%, a Dice coefficient of 93.97%, and a Jaccard index of 88.95%.
The method also showed superior performance on the ISIC 2017 dataset, achieving an accuracy of 94.63%, a Jaccard index of 79.23%, and a Dice coefficient of 87.26%.

The integration of ACM with ML techniques supports early and precise melanoma diagnosis by providing robust dermoscopic lesion image segmentation, which is critical for differentiating between melanoma and non-melanoma instances.

The study acknowledges that the abstract could be improved by addressing clinical relevance, outlining dataset sizes and potential biases, providing specific performance metric values, and detailing individual ML algorithms included in the ACM.
important_remarks: The paper's own abstract explicitly acknowledges limitations, stating the work should improve by addressing clinical relevance, outlining dataset sizes and potential biases, providing specific performance metric values, and detailing individual ML algorithms included in the ACM.

The study relies on the PH2 dataset for validation, which is a small, specialized dataset (200 images) with known selection bias (images with sharp edges), potentially limiting generalizability to broader clinical populations.

While the ISIC 2017 dataset is larger (2750 images), the paper does not provide specific metric values for the baseline models it claims to outperform, making direct quantitative comparison difficult for reviewers.

The methodology combines an Active Contour Model with multiple machine learning architectures (FCN, AlexNet, Mask R-CNN, etc.) for classification, but the integration details and specific roles of these algorithms within the ACM framework are noted as needing more detail.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed ACM over state-of-the-art techniques; comparisons are based solely on point estimates of accuracy, Dice, and Jaccard indices.

The paper does not report data leakage risks or rigorous cross-validation strategies beyond the standard train/test splits of the benchmark datasets, which is a concern for segmentation tasks where patch-level processing might introduce leakage.
